Is Claude Code Auto Memory Hogging Your Memory? A Cleanup & Diagnostics Guide
Auto Memory writes your feedback into
. It doesn't use RAM, but it does eat Context Window and disk space. The good news is the memory files are usually just KB in size — the real reason
balloons to hundreds of MB or even GB is the conversation jsonl files sitting in the same folder. Claude Code uses
(30 days by default) to auto-clean old session files, but if you just ran a massive session recently, waiting 30 days is a bit much. You can use
to fish out the big files instead.
A friend pinged me out of the blue the other day: "Hey Ray, my disk just lost 300–500 MB out of nowhere, and every time I launch Claude my Context drops way faster than usual. Any ideas?"
I had a pretty good guess. They'd been hammering Claude Code hard recently, so my first instinct was to check
. Sure enough, that folder alone had eaten 300+ MB — and that's tied to Claude Code's Auto Memory mechanism. So this post is about that.
I also wrote a previous post —
What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature

— covering the basics and how Auto Memory works. This one picks up from there to talk about what Auto Memory actually "occupies," what's really eating your disk and Context Window, and how to deal with it.
What does Auto Memory actually occupy?
Quick answer first — Auto Memory occupies two things, and neither of them is RAM:
Auto-loaded at the start of every conversation
Memory content is written as Markdown files in your Home directory
Heads up — when I say "memory" in this post I don't mean RAM. I mean Context Window and disk space.
So how do you find this
? It's just the index file inside your project's
. It lists all your memory files (Markdown files), one entry per line. Claude uses that index to find and read the corresponding memory content.
Claude Code Auto Memory file structure overview
Context Window: how does MEMORY.md eat tokens?
Auto Memory content lives as Markdown files on disk. Every time you start a new conversation, Claude Code auto-loads the content of
into the Context Window so Claude can reference those memories during the conversation.
doesn't get loaded in its entirety — there's a limit:
Auto Memory only loads the first 200 lines or first 25KB (whichever comes first) of

is over 200 lines or 25KB, the part past that threshold doesn't get loaded into Context at all. This is also why some people swear Claude was supposed to remember something — they clearly told it, Claude even confirmed it remembered — but it has no recollection later. It just never saw that memory entry.

Each line is just a one-sentence index pointing at the actual memory file. With this kind of setup, even if your memory grows to dozens of entries, only these index lines get loaded into Context Window at startup. The actual detail behind each entry only gets read when Claude needs it in the conversation.

---
name: UI changes must go through ui-ux-pro-max skill

Any change that touches **UI/UX** (layout, interactions, visuals, component design, Tailwind classes, component structure, etc.) **must go through the Skill tool by calling `ui-ux-pro-max:ui-ux-pro-max`** — don't edit directly.

**Why:** The user explicitly instructed: "If anything touches UI, you need to call UIUX SKILL Pro to do the adjustment, modification, or addition." Routing through the dedicated skill keeps consistency and quality.

**How to apply:**
- Check whether this change touches UI (any change to a .vue file's template/style block, Tailwind class change, new component, interaction flow change all count)
- UI-related → call `Skill({ skill: 'ui-ux-pro-max:ui-ux-pro-max', args: '<brief description of the change>' })`
- Not UI-related (only .ts service / types / composable logic) → handle it yourself
- Edge cases (e.g. composable affects UI behavior but isn't UI itself): use "does it ultimately touch a .vue file?" as the test
- Before calling, prep a clear description of what's needed (which screen, current issue, intended direction) so the skill can produce useful suggestions

Most of the time you'd ask the AI to organize this for you rather than hand-editing — the whole point of Auto Memory is letting the AI manage these memories. Doing it manually kind of defeats the purpose. That said, understanding how the structure and loading mechanism work is still useful when something goes wrong and you need to debug it.
Disk space: the real culprit isn't Auto Memory
Now the disk side — this is where most people misunderstand Auto Memory.
Let's first see how much Auto Memory itself actually takes up. Using one of my own projects as an example, the memory folder looks like this:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
folder adds up to 52KB. At this scale, you'll basically never need to clean it.
! So who's actually taking up all that space?
— Claude Code saves every single conversation as a

file, where each line is a JSON object containing your prompt, Claude's response, tool calls, tool results — everything written down. For long conversations or sessions with heavy file reads/writes, a single file can easily hit MB-level.
The biggest single conversation file is already up to 8 MB.

So how do I auto-clean these? Meet cleanupPeriodDays
OK, good news — there's a way to handle this. Claude Code has a built-in auto-cleanup mechanism that deletes session files older than 30 days when it starts up. The setting is called
only cleans conversation logs — it doesn't touch Auto Memory's memory files. Whatever memories you've written stay put. No need to worry.
If you want to change the retention period, add this to
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Now Claude Code will delete any jsonl older than 7 days on each startup. Minimum is 1 day; setting it to 0 throws an error.
If you don't want to keep any conversation log at all (privacy reasons or just saving space), you can disable transcript writes entirely:
, and Claude Code will stop writing conversation jsonl from then on. The trade-off: you also lose the ability to
back to past conversations. Your call which one you'd rather have.
handles the auto-cleanup, but if you just ran a huge session in the last couple weeks (like marathon subagent debugging), waiting 30 days is too long.

Opened `~/.claude/projects/` and noticed it keeps growing? The first instinct is usually to blame Auto Memory. Turns out Auto Memory doesn't eat your RAM at all — it actually eats Context Window (MEMORY.md loads only the first 200 lines or 25KB) and disk space. And the real culprit bloating that folder isn't memory files, it's conversation jsonl. This post walks through what Auto Memory actually consumes, the `cleanupPeriodDays` auto-cleanup mechanism, `CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY` for fully disabling transcript writes, and how to use `find` to clean out large files manually.
